title: Inside The Deep South's Disappearing City (Pine Bluff)
body: 20 years ago I worked for Footlocker.  My DM called me and told me he needed me to go run a store for a week.  That store was in Pine Bluff.  The first day there customers were running through the mall screaming.  Then I heard “they’re shooting again”.  I said “Again?”.  Longest week of my life.
author: @jameshardin8870

title: Inside The Deep South's Disappearing City (Pine Bluff)
body: I was a police officer there in the late 90s. It was heartbreaking to see the massive decline. There was a violence and drug problem back then, too, but Main St. and other areas weren't a ghost town, and the mall was thriving. Lots of good people. Even a lot of the criminals just trying to make it in a terrible economy or battling addictions. I wish the city well.
author: @RowanRiven

title: Inside The Deep South's Disappearing City (Pine Bluff)
body: My family goes back generations in Pine Bluff and although it is home, our biggest blessing was our dad leaving for the military once he could. It is sad every time I visit, but still holds so much nostalgia.
author: @everrick1

title: Inside The Deep South's Disappearing City (Pine Bluff)
body: There are so many layers at play.  The downtown was replaced by the mall, and the mall was replaced by strip centers along  I530, and instead of gainful employment, the casinos siphon off what's left of the city's income. Corrupt city councils, through and through.

body: Pine Bluff is my hometown, but I left at the first opportunity. It wasn't that bad when I was growing up in the 80s and 90s, but now it's like visiting the ruins of a once great city and the people who could turn things around left 30 years ago.

title: Inside The Deep South's Disappearing City (Pine Bluff)
body: 5:40 is the exact problem in every corrupt small town. They not solving no violent crimes. The town entire revenue is catch and release and re-catch again. Then the rest is unlawful stops hoping to you have an active warrant or issuing a traffic ticket. That’s 80% of all small towns revenue especially in the south.

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body: I lived in P.B. for years.  I have NEVER seen a town change so quickly!  I was around from the 70s to 20s and still have family there.  The quickest change over was from 1990 to 2000. From there constantly going down. At one point Main St. down from the court house was closed over a year because of crumbling buildings into the street.
author: @ralemc1960

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body: I’m from and still in Arkansas, no such thing as “bitter cold winters” here bro. But everything else was pretty spot on.
author: @zg5761

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body: I lived in Pine Bluff from 1989 to 2015 and it wasn’t that bad at all. We have a beautiful University of Arkansas at Pine Bluff where I attended and graduated. When I lived there things were different and although I moved away in 2015 it will always be home for me because so many good things happen to me there. I know Pine Bluff is going through a lot these days but I pray that it gets better and everyone work together.
author: @pertinawhite9175

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body:
I'm going out on a wild card here but I'm pretty sure factories and businesses closing down, which play a vital role in generating revenue and providing income, play a major role in laying out the red carpet for gangs and drugs to take over, which appeal to disparaged people.

You don't really see either proliferating in highly prosperous towns and cities

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body: First off, contrary to what this commentator thinks, many parts of Ark. are good places to live.  Of course, one cannot control the weather and tornadoes are somewhat common...as in East TX and Eastern OK.  There are a few things he mentioned that were definitely caused by economic and leadership problems in Pine Bluff.  But about 6 of his reasons were basically due to one factor.  Pine Bluff population is just about 80% black and when you put black, unemployment, drugs and gangs together in one town, it will definitely be a crappy town.  My wife is from Pine Bluff and we still have relatives there.  I have been spending Christmas vacations there for going on 40 yrs. now and I watched as the town went down.  My mother in law is one of only 3 whites that live on the street she lives on.  She has been robbed and assaulted 3 times in the past 5 yrs. but refuses to leave her "home".  When my wife and I are there, we often take our dogs for a walk and when we do, not only are our very protective dogs with us, we are also both "packing".  PINE BLUFF SUCKS!
author: @reb1050

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body: 🤣he said I don't know what is going on in that town but they need to knock it off..4real 💯
author: @uknw1952

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body: I lived in Pine Bluff from 1979 to 1988 and it was not a bad place to live it had crime but no where near the astronomical numbers you see today. It is my contention that Pine Bluff turned into crime bluff shortly after hurricane Katrina ripped New Orleans apart. The displaced residents fled to 2 planes notably. Houston and all parts of eastern Texas and Arkansas (mainly the south east) if you go back and look at those areas crime spikes starting with 2004 you'll see clearly that displaced residents of New Orleans helped the area's crime numbers tremendously💁🏾‍♂️
author: @pharcyde1us1

author: @geraldineduncan9212

title: Top 10 Reasons NOT to move to Pine Bluff, AR
body:
Briggs, I was born and raised in Pine Bluff. Growing up in the 60's and early 70's it wasn't that bad. A lot of great memories living there. However, my senior year of high school Pine Bluff was listed as the city (yes, it was a city at this time) with the highest per capita murder rate on the.... wait for it.....PLANET. It was worse there than literally anywhere on the planet. I escaped.....I mean moved away to the DFW area in 83 and haven't looked back. Even in high school it became obvious that if you weren't born into the old money or married into the old money, you weren't getting anywhere in this town. You would have a minimum wage job or maybe score a job at one of the two paper mills in town, or one of the two railroads, or one of the two state prisons, or maybe a job out at the Pine Bluff Arsenal at NCTR (National Center for Toxicogical Research) They made ALL of the chemical agents the US Armed Forces have ever used. All of it. Now, I believe only one paper mill is left operational. The prisons are still there. NCTR is still there but the two railroads have now been merged into the Union Pacific and almost all operations have been moved to North Little Rock. It's just terribly sad. I still have family (in laws) that live there. That's the only reason I even keep up. On a visit back once in the late 80's I learned it was so bad that the local movie theaters no longer had evening shows. Too dangerous, nobody goes out much after dark In the mid 80's, 60 Minutes did a story on PB after it was listed as the worst city in the US to live in for the third year in a row. Your not wrong at all on your list, but you could do a longer one if people aren't convinced.
Love your videos. Keep it up. As for Pine Bluff, you have just scratched the surface. There are a lot of historical things good about PB, just not enough to outweigh the bad.
author: @txrazorback6357
